name: Blocking sleep in async test context
description: time.sleep() called in a test file that also uses asyncio — blocks the event loop, slows the suite, and causes stalls if called from an async def or pytest-asyncio test.

bad_example.code:
async def test_rate_window_buckets(store):
    await store.record(event_a)
    time.sleep(0.01)  # Ensure different timestamp buckets
    await store.record(event_b)
    results = await store.get_buckets()
    assert len(results) == 2
bad_example.why_bad: time.sleep() blocks the event loop inside an async test. asyncio.sleep() (with await) is required here. Also: relying on wall-clock time for timestamp bucketing is brittle and race-prone.

good_example.code:
async def test_rate_window_buckets(store, freezer):
    await store.record(event_a)
    freezer.tick(timedelta(milliseconds=10))
    await store.record(event_b)
    results = await store.get_buckets()
    assert len(results) == 2
good_example.why_better: Use freezegun or time-travel fixtures to advance time without blocking the loop.

detection.false_positive_note: time.sleep() is fine in sync test helpers that are not called from async tests. Check whether the function or its callers are async.

source: deep_think_mcp scan 2026-06-03; found in tests/test_health.py:297, tests/root/test_tool_invoker.py:356, mqtt/tests/test_feedback_store.py:225,445
